class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Health Psychology and the Biopsychosocial Model
learning_objectives: Define health psychology and its contribution to health care
Explain the biopsychosocial model as an alternative to the biomedical model
Describe the leading causes of death and how behavioral factors contribute

lecture_notes.introduction: Health psychology examines how psychological factors influence health, illness, and health care. The leading causes of death are now largely behavioral: smoking, diet, physical inactivity, and alcohol.
key_concepts: Biomedical model vs. biopsychosocial model
Behavioral risk factors account for approximately 40% of premature mortality
Health disparities across socioeconomic status, race, and gender
Health psychologist roles: research, clinical practice, public health, policy
Mind-body connection: psychoneuroimmunology
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze CDC mortality data: identify which top-10 causes of death have modifiable behavioral risk factors

review.value: Health and illness are determined by biological (genetics, physiology), psychological (behavior, cognition, emotion), and social (culture, SES, support) factors interacting
Study of how psychological factors (stress, mood) influence the nervous and immune systems, affecting susceptibility to illness

learning_objectives: Describe the physiological stress response and allostatic load
Compare problem-focused and emotion-focused coping strategies
Explain how chronic stress contributes to cardiovascular disease and immune suppression

lecture_notes.introduction: Stress is the most extensively studied pathway between psychological experience and physical health. Understanding stress mechanisms reveals how the mind affects the body.
key_concepts: Selye's general adaptation syndrome: alarm, resistance, exhaustion
Allostatic load: cumulative wear from repeated stress activation
Lazarus and Folkman's transactional model: primary and secondary appraisal
Problem-focused vs. emotion-focused coping; when each is adaptive
Social support as a stress buffer: main effect and buffering hypotheses
lecture_notes.demonstration: Stress appraisal exercise: students reappraise a stressful academic scenario and measure changes in perceived stress

review.value: Cumulative physiological cost of chronic stress adaptation: elevated cortisol, blood pressure, inflammation, and metabolic dysregulation
Social support protects health primarily during high-stress periods by providing resources for coping (buffering hypothesis)

class_schedule.topic: Health Behavior Change: Models and Interventions
learning_objectives: Compare the Health Belief Model, Theory of Planned Behavior, and Transtheoretical Model
Describe motivational interviewing and its evidence base
Evaluate the effectiveness of behavior change interventions

lecture_notes.introduction: Changing health behaviors is one of the most important and most difficult challenges in health psychology. Theory-driven interventions are more effective than intuition-based approaches.
key_concepts: Health Belief Model: susceptibility, severity, benefits, barriers, cues to action, self-efficacy
Theory of Planned Behavior: attitudes, subjective norms, perceived behavioral control
Transtheoretical Model: precontemplation, contemplation, preparation, action, maintenance
Motivational interviewing: express empathy, develop discrepancy, roll with resistance, support self-efficacy
Implementation intentions: 'if-then' planning for behavior change
lecture_notes.demonstration: Practice motivational interviewing: OARS skills (open questions, affirmations, reflections, summaries) in triads

review.value: Client-centered, directive method for eliciting behavior change by exploring and resolving ambivalence; strong evidence for substance use, diet, and exercise
Specific if-then plans (e.g., 'If it is Monday at 7am, then I will go for a run') double the likelihood of following through on health behavior goals

class_schedule.topic: Pain, Chronic Illness, and Patient Adjustment
learning_objectives: Explain the gate control theory of pain and psychological pain management
Describe the psychological impact of chronic illness diagnosis and adjustment
Discuss adherence to medical treatment and factors that influence it

lecture_notes.introduction: Chronic pain and illness are not purely biomedical experiences. Psychological factors profoundly influence pain perception, illness adjustment, and treatment adherence.
key_concepts: Gate control theory: psychological factors modulate pain signals at the spinal cord level
Biopsychosocial model of pain: sensory, affective, cognitive, behavioral components
Cognitive-behavioral pain management: cognitive restructuring, behavioral activation, relaxation
Stages of adjustment to chronic illness: denial, anxiety, adaptation
Treatment adherence: 50% of patients with chronic conditions are non-adherent
lecture_notes.demonstration: Cold pressor task: demonstrate how distraction and reappraisal modify pain tolerance
setup_requirements: Cold water setup for pain tolerance demonstration

review.value: Melzack and Wall: pain signals can be modulated at the spinal cord level by descending signals from the brain; explains how attention, emotion, and cognition influence pain
Approximately 50% non-adherence for chronic conditions; predicted by regimen complexity, side effects, poor provider communication, and patient beliefs about illness

class_schedule.topic: Cardiovascular Health, Cancer, and Immune Function
learning_objectives: Describe psychological risk factors for cardiovascular disease
Explain the relationship between psychological factors and cancer progression
Discuss how stress and psychological interventions affect immune function

lecture_notes.introduction: The leading causes of death -- heart disease and cancer -- have significant psychological components. Health psychology research has identified both risk factors and protective interventions.
key_concepts: Type A behavior and hostility as cardiovascular risk factors
Depression as an independent risk factor for coronary heart disease
Psychological factors in cancer: stress, coping, and quality of life (not causation)
Psychoneuroimmunology: stress reduces NK cell activity and vaccine response
Psychological interventions improve quality of life and may affect disease progression
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze a prospective cohort study linking hostility to cardiovascular outcomes: identify strengths and limitations

review.value: The toxic component of Type A: cynical hostility is independently associated with coronary heart disease, controlling for traditional risk factors
Chronic stress suppresses cellular immunity (NK cells, T-cells); loneliness and caregiving stress are particularly potent immune suppressors

class_schedule.topic: Health Promotion, Prevention, and Health Disparities
learning_objectives: Analyze the social determinants of health and health disparities
Discuss the role of health psychologists in public health policy
readings: Taylor, Ch. 15; Marmot, The Health Gap (selections)

lecture_notes.introduction: Health psychology's ultimate goal is prevention. Addressing health disparities requires understanding how social determinants of health create unequal opportunities for well-being.
key_concepts: Primary, secondary, and tertiary prevention
Social determinants of health: income, education, neighborhood, discrimination
Health literacy and its impact on health outcomes
Community-based participatory research (CBPR)
Positive psychology and health: optimism, purpose, and resilience as protective factors
lecture_notes.demonstration: Map health disparities in a community using publicly available health data and identify intervention targets

review.value: Conditions in which people are born, grow, live, work, and age; account for 30-55% of health outcomes, more than health care (10-20%) or individual behavior (30%)
The ability to obtain, process, and understand basic health information needed to make appropriate health decisions; low literacy is associated with worse outcomes across conditions

questions.question: Explain the difference between allostatic load and the acute stress response, and describe two specific physiological consequences of chronic allostatic load.
questions.answer: The acute stress response (fight-or-flight) is an adaptive, short-term physiological mobilization: cortisol and adrenaline prepare the body for action, then levels return to baseline. Allostatic load is the cumulative physiological cost of chronic or repeated stress activation where the system fails to return fully to baseline. Two consequences: (1) Chronic cortisol elevation damages hippocampal neurons, impairing memory and stress regulation (a vicious cycle). (2) Sustained inflammatory cytokine production contributes to atherosclerosis and cardiovascular disease by damaging blood vessel walls.

rubric.key: Distinguishes acute from chronic stress mechanisms
Names two specific physiological consequences with mechanisms

questions.question: A client wants to start exercising regularly but has failed multiple times. Using the Transtheoretical Model, determine their likely stage and describe three stage-matched strategies.
questions.answer: Having tried and failed multiple times, the client is likely in the contemplation or preparation stage (ambivalent but interested). Stage-matched strategies: (1) Decisional balance: list pros and cons of exercising to tip the balance toward action. (2) Self-efficacy building: start with small, achievable goals (10-minute walks) to build confidence through mastery experiences. (3) Implementation intentions: create specific if-then plans ('If it is Tuesday at 6pm, then I will walk for 15 minutes in my neighborhood'). For contemplation specifically, motivational interviewing to explore ambivalence would be appropriate.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies likely stage with reasoning

questions.question: Using gate control theory, explain how a soldier can be severely wounded in battle but report no pain until hours later.
questions.answer: Gate control theory proposes that a gating mechanism in the spinal cord modulates pain signals before they reach the brain. Descending signals from the brain (top-down) can close the gate, reducing pain perception. In battle, several factors close the gate: (1) Intense attentional focus on survival diverts processing resources away from pain. (2) Endogenous opioid release (endorphins) during extreme stress directly inhibits pain transmission at the spinal cord. (3) Emotional arousal and adrenaline activate descending inhibitory pathways. (4) The meaning of the wound (survival, heroism) can modify the affective component of pain. When the threat passes and these factors diminish, the gate opens and pain is perceived.

rubric.key: Correctly applies gate control theory to the scenario
Identifies specific mechanisms (endorphins, attention, descending inhibition)
Notes the role of context and meaning in pain perception

questions.question: Explain why approximately 50% of patients with chronic conditions do not adhere to prescribed treatments. Identify three evidence-based strategies for improving adherence.
questions.answer: Non-adherence results from multiple interacting factors: complex regimens, side effects, asymptomatic conditions (no perceived benefit), poor provider communication, health literacy barriers, cost, and illness beliefs that conflict with treatment. Three evidence-based strategies: (1) Simplify the regimen: reduce dosing frequency, use combination pills, align with existing routines. (2) Motivational interviewing: explore ambivalence about treatment, elicit the patient's own reasons for adherence. (3) Electronic reminders and self-monitoring: text message reminders and pill tracking apps increase adherence by 15-20% in meta-analyses. Additional: improve health literacy through teach-back method.

rubric.key: Strategies are specific and supported by research

major_assignments.title: Health Behavior Change Program Design
major_assignments.description: Design a theory-driven health behavior change intervention for a specific population and health behavior (e.g., smoking cessation for pregnant women, exercise promotion for older adults with diabetes). Include theoretical justification, intervention components, implementation plan, and evaluation strategy.
objectives: Apply health behavior change theories to a real-world health problem
Design a multi-component intervention grounded in research evidence
Address health disparities and cultural factors in intervention design
deliverables: Logic model connecting theory to intervention components to outcomes
Evaluation plan with specific outcome measures

criteria: Clear theoretical framework guiding intervention design
Theory-component alignment documented in logic model
Power analysis and sample size justification
Strong theoretical foundation with clear logic model
Detailed and culturally responsive intervention design
Rigorous evaluation plan with valid outcome measures

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of health psychology concepts and applications

journal_critiques.description: Critical analysis of published health psychology research articles

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering stress, coping, and behavior change theories

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected. In-class demonstrations and skills practice cannot be made up.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. The intervention proposal will not be accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of concepts is encouraged. All written assignments must be independently authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original with proper citations. Health data used must be from publicly available sources.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to health behavior databases, university library, and word processing software.
